title: VA Withdraws Controversial Medication Rule After Veteran Backlash
excerpt: Secretary Collins confirms the interim final rule on disability ratings under medication will not be enforced -- ever. Here's what happened and what it means for your claim.
body:
The Department of Veterans Affairs has officially pulled the plug on a rule that would have fundamentally changed how disability ratings are calculated for veterans taking medication.

The interim final rule, titled 'Evaluative Rating: Impact of Medication,' was published in the Federal Register in February 2026. It stated that disability levels should be based on how well a veteran functions while on medication -- not on the underlying impairment itself. The implications were immediate and severe: a veteran rated at 70% for PTSD whose symptoms improved with medication could have been reevaluated and reduced to 30% or lower.

## :gavel: What the Rule Said

The rule would have required VA raters to evaluate disabilities based on the veteran's functional capacity with medication, rather than the severity of the underlying condition. For veterans on psychiatric medications, pain management regimens, or chronic disease treatments, this represented an existential threat to their ratings.

The Veterans of Foreign Wars, Disabled American Veterans, and multiple VSOs issued formal statements opposing the rule within 48 hours of publication. The DAV called it 'a fundamental departure from the principles that have guided VA disability compensation for decades.'

## :shield: The Withdrawal

On February 19, 2026, VA Secretary Doug Collins announced that the rule 'will not be enforced at any time in the future.' Deputy Secretary Tanya Bradsher confirmed to Task & Purpose that the department has no implementation plans.

> [!important] If you received any communication from VA referencing this rule or requesting a reevaluation based on medication effectiveness, contact your VSO immediately. The rule is dead, but automated processes may not have been fully updated.

## :target: What This Means for Current Claims

For veterans with pending claims or recent C&P exams: the withdrawal means your disability should continue to be rated based on the underlying condition, not how well medication controls symptoms. If a recent C&P examiner focused heavily on medication effectiveness, you may want to flag this to your representative.

For veterans considering new claims: nothing changes. The rating schedule continues to evaluate the severity of the condition itself. If your condition warrants a higher rating, file.

## :search: The Bigger Picture

This episode highlights a pattern: VA proposes cost-cutting regulatory changes, veterans organizations mobilize, and the rule gets pulled. But the speed of this cycle -- from publication to withdrawal in under a week -- suggests the advocacy infrastructure is working. The question is whether the next attempt will come with less warning.

The GAO's February 2026 report (GAO-26-108844) found that VA decisions on veterans' claims 'continue to be based, in part, on outdated criteria.' That report may provide the policy foundation for future rating schedule changes -- changes that could go either way for veterans.
author: Chris Combs

title: VA Claims Backlog Drops Below 100K for First Time Since 2020
excerpt: The department reports a 63% backlog reduction since January 2025 -- but PACT Act claims and rating schedule updates could reverse the trend.
body:
The VA announced this week that the disability compensation and pension claims backlog has fallen below 100,000 pending claims for the first time since May 2020. The department credits automation improvements, increased staffing, and streamlined processing for PACT Act toxic exposure claims.

## :chart: The Numbers

The backlog -- defined as claims pending longer than 125 days -- dropped from approximately 270,000 in January 2025 to under 97,000 as of late February 2026. That represents a 63% reduction in just over a year.

But the headline number obscures some complexity. The total number of claims filed continues to increase, driven largely by PACT Act presumptive conditions. VA processed over 2.4 million claims in fiscal year 2025. The backlog reduction reflects faster processing, not fewer claims.

## :search: What's Driving the Improvement

**Automation:** VA's internal AI systems, including the 'Exam Data Science' tool that reviews C&P exams for completeness, have reduced manual review time for straightforward claims.

**PACT Act presumptives:** Ironically, presumptive claims are easier to process than claims requiring individual nexus determinations. Once VA concedes the presumption, the only question is the rating percentage.

**Staffing:** VA hired approximately 4,000 additional claims processors in 2025, with most now through training and producing at full capacity.

> [!warning] A backlog under 100K does not mean your individual claim will be processed faster. Complex claims -- especially those involving secondary conditions, aggravation theories, or contested C&P exams -- still routinely take 6-12 months. The backlog reduction disproportionately benefits straightforward presumptive claims.

## :target: What Could Reverse the Trend

The remaining four body system rating schedule updates, expected in fiscal year 2026, could trigger a wave of reevaluations and new claims. When VA updated the musculoskeletal and mental health schedules in previous years, both new claims and appeals spiked.

Additionally, veterans advocacy groups are pushing for expanded PACT Act presumptives -- particularly for conditions linked to burn pit exposure that aren't currently on the presumptive list. Each expansion adds volume.

## :tools: Action Item

If you've been waiting to file, the processing environment has never been better. Intent to File (VA Form 21-0966) locks in your effective date while you build your evidence package. File it today, then take up to a year to submit the full claim.
author: Chris Combs

title: 2026 VA Disability Pay Rates: 2.8% COLA Increase Now in Effect
excerpt: December 2025 COLA adjustment means higher monthly payments for all rated veterans. Here's the updated rate table and what it means for your check.
body:
The 2.8% cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) for VA disability compensation is now reflected in monthly payments. The increase, effective December 1, 2025, applies to all veterans receiving disability compensation regardless of rating percentage.

## :money: Updated Monthly Rates (Single Veteran, No Dependents)

| Rating | 2025 Rate | 2026 Rate | Monthly Increase |
|--------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| 10% | $171.23 | $176.03 | +$4.80 |
| 20% | $338.49 | $347.97 | +$9.48 |
| 30% | $524.31 | $539.00 | +$14.69 |
| 40% | $755.28 | $776.43 | +$21.15 |
| 50% | $1,075.16 | $1,105.27 | +$30.11 |
| 60% | $1,361.88 | $1,400.01 | +$38.13 |
| 70% | $1,716.28 | $1,764.34 | +$48.06 |
| 80% | $1,995.01 | $2,050.87 | +$55.86 |
| 90% | $2,241.91 | $2,304.68 | +$62.77 |
| 100% | $3,737.85 | $3,842.51 | +$104.66 |

Rates for veterans with dependents are higher. The VA rate tables at va.gov/disability/compensation-rates/ have the complete breakdown.

## :search: How COLA Works

VA disability compensation COLA is tied to the same Social Security COLA formula. The adjustment is based on the Consumer Price Index for Urban Wage Earners and Clerical Workers (CPI-W) from the third quarter of the prior year compared to the third quarter of the year before that.

The 2.8% increase for 2026 is lower than the 3.2% increase in 2025 and significantly lower than the 8.7% increase in 2023 that followed the post-pandemic inflation spike. The trend line suggests COLA increases will continue moderating toward the 2-3% historical average.

> [!tip] If your January or February 2026 payment did not reflect the 2.8% increase, check your payment history on va.gov. Some veterans on direct deposit saw delayed implementation. If the increase is not reflected by March, contact the VA benefits hotline at 800-827-1000.

## :target: Strategic Note

COLA adjustments apply automatically -- you don't need to do anything. But they're a reminder that even a small increase in your rating percentage produces compounding benefits over time. The difference between 60% and 70% is $364/month at 2026 rates, or $4,368 per year. Over a 30-year period, that single 10% increase is worth over $130,000 in additional compensation -- before future COLA adjustments.
author: Chris Combs

title: GAO Report: VA Still Using Outdated Criteria for Disability Decisions
excerpt: New Government Accountability Office report finds 4 of 15 body systems still running on decades-old rating criteria. Which ones affect your claim.
body:
The Government Accountability Office released report GAO-26-108844 in February 2026, finding that VA disability decisions 'continue to be based, in part, on outdated criteria' despite years of rating schedule modernization efforts.

## :search: What the Report Found

VA has completed rating schedule updates for 11 of 15 body systems, but 4 remain on criteria that in some cases date back decades. The GAO found that the outdated systems create inconsistency in how similar conditions are rated and may disadvantage veterans whose conditions fall under the older criteria.

The report does not specify which 4 body systems remain outdated, but VA officials have publicly stated that updates for the remaining systems are planned for fiscal year 2026.

## :gavel: Why This Matters

Outdated rating criteria can cut both ways. Some veterans benefit from older criteria that rate conditions more generously. Others are disadvantaged by criteria that don't account for modern medical understanding of their conditions.

When VA updated the musculoskeletal body system criteria, some diagnostic codes saw expanded rating possibilities while others were tightened. The mental health rating schedule update in 2023 was largely seen as beneficial to veterans, expanding the criteria for higher ratings.

> [!important] If you have a condition rated under criteria that haven't been updated, keep an eye on VA announcements. When a body system update takes effect, it can create opportunities for increased ratings -- but also risks of proposed reductions. Claims filed before the effective date of a change are generally evaluated under whichever criteria (old or new) are more favorable to the veteran.

## :tools: What You Can Do Now

Review your rating decision letter. It will reference specific diagnostic codes. Cross-reference those codes against the current 38 CFR Part 4 to see when your body system was last updated. If your condition falls under a recently updated system, consider whether the new criteria might support a higher rating than you currently have.

Our 38 CFR Navigator AI tool can help you look up your specific diagnostic code and understand how the current rating criteria apply to your condition.
author: Chris Combs

title: PACT Act Update: Where We Stand 3 Years In
excerpt: Over 1.2 million veterans have filed PACT Act claims since August 2022. Approval rates, common conditions, and what's still missing from the presumptive list.
body:
Three years after the PACT Act became law, the numbers tell a story of both success and unfinished business. Over 1.2 million toxic exposure claims have been filed, and VA reports that the majority of straightforward presumptive claims are being processed within the 125-day standard.

## :chart: By the Numbers

Since August 2022, PACT Act claims have accounted for a significant portion of all new VA disability claims. The most commonly filed conditions include:

- **Respiratory conditions** (sinusitis, rhinitis, asthma) linked to burn pit and airborne hazard exposure
- **Various cancers** on the presumptive list (bladder, head/neck, reproductive cancers)
- **Constrictive bronchiolitis** and other rare lung diseases
- **Agent Orange conditions** for veterans deployed to Thailand and other newly recognized locations

Approval rates for PACT Act presumptive claims are running higher than the overall VA claims approval rate, which is expected -- the entire point of presumptive service connection is to eliminate the nexus requirement.

## :search: What's Working

The concession framework for presumptives is functioning as intended. Veterans filing for conditions on the presumptive list with documented deployment to covered locations are getting approved without individual nexus letters. This is a fundamental improvement over the pre-PACT process, where burn pit veterans had to individually prove causation for each condition.

## :shield: What's Still Missing

Veterans advocacy organizations continue to push for expansion of the presumptive list. Several conditions with strong epidemiological links to burn pit exposure -- including certain autoimmune conditions and neurological disorders -- are not yet on the list.

The individual claim pathway (non-presumptive) remains available for conditions not on the list, but requires the traditional nexus letter and individual causation evidence. This is where many veterans get stuck.

> [!tip] Even if your specific condition isn't on the PACT Act presumptive list, the Airborne Hazards and Open Burn Pit Registry can strengthen a non-presumptive claim. If you haven't registered yet, do so at va.gov/health-care/health-needs-conditions/health-issues-related-to-service-era/burn-pit-exposure/.

## :target: Upcoming Milestones

The PACT Act mandated phased implementation of benefits through 2026. The final expansion phases include additional Agent Orange presumptives for veterans who served in specific locations and timeframes. Check va.gov/pact for the latest eligibility criteria.

If you served in Southwest Asia, the Gulf War theater, or other covered locations after August 2, 1990, you likely have PACT Act eligibility. The only question is whether your conditions qualify.
author: Chris Combs
